title: Mark 9v33-37.
scripture_ref: Matthew 18v1-9. Mark 9v33-37. The disciples argue part 2
scripture_text: ‘’At that time the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Who, then, is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?”He called a little child to him, and placed the child among them And he said “Truly I tell you, unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven. Therefore, whoever takes the lowly position of this child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven And whoever welcomes one such child in my name welcomes me “If anyone causes one of these little ones—those who believe in me—to stumble, it would be better for them to have a large millstone hung around their neck and to be drowned in the depths of the sea. Woe to the world because of the things that cause people to stumble! Such things must come, but woe to the person through whom they come! If your hand or your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life maimed or crippled than to have two hands or two feet and be thrown into eternal fire And if your eye causes you to stumble, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into the fire of hell.’’
devotional:
‘’They came to Capernaum. When he was in the house, he asked them “What were you arguing about on the road?” But they kept quiet because on the way they had argued about who was the greatest. Sitting down, Jesus called the Twelve and said “Anyone who wants to be first must be the very last, and the servant of all.” He took a little child whom he placed among them. Taking the child in his arms, he said to them, Whoever welcomes one of these little children in my name welcomes me; and whoever welcomes me does not welcome me but the one who sent me.”

Questions about the question.

Mark does not tell us what they were arguing about, but Matthew and Luke do. After some probing by Jesus the disciples eventually ask 'Which of them would be the greatest?' We want to ask two questions about the disciples' request.

a) What does it mean?

b) Why did they ask it?

What does it mean?

When the disciples eventually asked Jesus 'Which of them would be the greatest?' this was much more than a question, it was a window into their hearts and minds. It revealed an identity problem. These men believed that a person's identify, value and worth is evaluated by how 'great' they are. They are looking for significance and importance, they long for position and prestige, and they wanted to know who was 'top dog.' Because of their insecurity they were caught up in the pursuit of status, the acclaim of others, always comparing themselves with others. The insecurity that drove this question would make them a liability to the kingdom of God, it would cause them to abuse positions of power and manipulate others to meet their egotistical desires, so Jesus needed to address it.

Why did they ask it at this time? To help us understand why the question 'Who is the greatest?' became a significant issue in the hearts of the disciples at this time we need to look at some of the background.

They used wrong categories to evaluate each other. The question they asked indicates that they viewed each other in terms of 'failure' or 'success.' Peter, James and John had been up the mountain where they saw Jesus transfigured. The other nine were at the foot of the mountain trying to hide their inability to cast out a demon by arguing with the teachers of the law. Were the three on the mountaintop more successful or more spiritual than the other nine? Did they start to compare themselves with one another?

Seeing people in terms of 'failure' or 'success' is like using a ruler to measure water, or scales to measure distance, it doesn't work. 'Failure' and 'success' evaluations will encourage shame or pride, two negative and dangerous emotions. The question 'Who is the greatest?' means that we have become judges of others, determining their worth in the light of how we see ourselves. They avoided listening to what Jesus had to say by becoming self obsessed. When we look at the question 'Who is the greatest?' in its immediate context it is very frightening. Look at Mark 9:30-32 'They left that place and passed through Galilee. Jesus did not want anyone to know where they were, because he was teaching his disciples. He said to them. 'The Son of man is going to be betrayed into the hands of men. They will kill him and after three days he will rise again, but they did not understand what he meant and were afraid to ask him about it.'

This is the second and shortest of three passion announcements in this Gospel (see 8:31, 10:33-34). The important thing for us to notice is that the disciples 'did not understand what he meant and were afraid to ask him about it.' The refusal to accept and the inability to understand Jesus' teaching about His cross encouraged the disciples to think egotistically. It is possible that the desire for greatness was a reaction to the cross and a diversionary device to prevent them from thinking about it.
prayer: Father please will you change my understanding of greatness.
html:
Mark 9v33-37.

Study Text: Matthew 18v1-9. Mark 9v33-37. The disciples argue part 2
‘’At that time the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Who, then, is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?”He called a little child to him, and placed the child among them And he said “Truly I tell you, unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven. Therefore, whoever takes the lowly position of this child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven And whoever welcomes one such child in my name welcomes me “If anyone causes one of these little ones—those who believe in me—to stumble, it would be better for them to have a large millstone hung around their neck and to be drowned in the depths of the sea. Woe to the world because of the things that cause people to stumble! Such things must come, but woe to the person through whom they come! If your hand or your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life maimed or crippled than to have two hands or two feet and be thrown into eternal fire And if your eye causes you to stumble, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into the fire of hell.’’

‘’They came to Capernaum. When he was in the house, he asked them “What were you arguing about on the road?” But they kept quiet because on the way they had argued about who was the greatest. Sitting down, Jesus called the Twelve and said “Anyone who wants to be first must be the very last, and the servant of all.” He took a little child whom he placed among them. Look at Mark 9:30-32 'They left that place and passed through Galilee. Jesus did not want anyone to know where they were, because he was teaching his disciples. He said to them. 'The Son of man is going to be betrayed into the hands of men. They will kill him and after three days he will rise again, but they did not understand what he meant and were afraid to ask him about it.'

Prayer: Father please will you change my understanding of greatness.
Bible in a year reading plan: -1 Samuel 25:32-30:10
